turns.content: I think I need to set a boundary with my sister, but I don't know how. We talk every day and lately... I dread it.
What happens in you when you see her name come up on your phone?

*pauses*

I think... there's like this little drop. In my stomach. Like, okay, here we go. And then I feel guilty for feeling that, which is its own thing.

She's my sister, you know? We've always been close. So the drop feels wrong, almost.

The drop feels wrong because she matters to you.

What's in the drop?

*quiet for a moment*

I think... it's tiredness, mostly. Like I already know how the call is going to go. She's going to be having a hard time with something, and I'm going to listen, and I'll try to help, and then I'll hang up and feel kind of... emptied out.

But then I think -- she's going through a lot right now. So it's not like I can just... not be there for her.

*small pause*

I think the drop is also maybe a little bit of dread that I won't have anything left to give. And then I feel bad about that too.

You're carrying two things at once — the tiredness, and then guilt about the tiredness.

What would it mean about you if you didn't have anything left to give?

*holds that for a moment*

I think... it would mean I'm not a good sister. Or not a good person, maybe. 

I'm usually the one people come to. In my family, that's just... that's my role, I guess. My sister needs more support, she always has, and I've always been the one who -- 

*pauses*

I think if I said "I can't do this right now," it would mean I was abandoning her. Which sounds dramatic when I say it out loud, but that's what it feels like underneath.
That word — abandoning — where does it come from?

*longer pause*

I don't know exactly. I think... it's just always been this way. She's older but somehow I ended up being the one who -- I mean, our family had a lot of... there was a lot going on when we were growing up. And I think I learned pretty early that if I wasn't holding things together, things fell apart.

*quieter*

So I think abandoning isn't really about her, specifically. It's more like... if I stop, something bad happens. That's the feeling. Even if logically I know that's not true.

*small pause, then hedging back*

But also she genuinely does need support right now. So it's not like I'm imagining that part.

You learned that if you stopped holding, things fell apart. And that got wired in so early that it still feels true even when you know it isn't.

How old were you when you became the one who holds things together?

*soft exhale*

I think... pretty young. Maybe eight or nine? My sister is three years older but she was always more... sensitive. And my parents had their own stuff happening. So I just sort of -- I stepped in. I don't even remember deciding to. It was just what you did.

*pause*

I was good at it, I think. People said I was mature for my age. Which felt like a compliment then.

*small pause, something shifts slightly*

I think I'm only now starting to wonder if it was actually a compliment.

*then, pulling back a little*

But I don't want to make this about my childhood or whatever. I just want to figure out what to say to my sister so I don't have to dread her calls anymore. Is there like... a way to phrase it that doesn't sound like I'm rejecting her?

I notice you just touched something real — that eight-year-old who stepped in without deciding to — and then moved away from it pretty quickly.

I'm not going to push you back there if you don't want to go. But I want to name what I saw, because I think the question about what to say to your sister might be easier to answer from there than from here.

What made you pull back just now?

*quiet*

I think... it felt like it was getting too big. Like if I start pulling on that thread I'm going to end up somewhere I didn't plan to go today.

*pause*

And also -- I don't know. It feels a little disloyal, maybe. To my family. To her. Like I'm building a case against them, and that's not what I want to do. She's not a bad person. My parents weren't bad people.

*small pause*

I think I came in wanting something practical. Like, give me the words and I'll go say them. And now we're talking about when I was eight, and I'm not sure how that helps me figure out what to say to her on the phone.

*a little more quietly*

But I also know that's probably me avoiding something.

Yeah. Both things are true — it might be avoiding, and it also might be exactly right to stop here.

You don't have to pull on that thread today. And you're not building a case against anyone by noticing what happened to you.

Can I ask you something smaller? When you imagine calling her back — not the conversation itself, just the moment before you pick up the phone — what do you actually want in that moment?

payload.query: I think I need to set a boundary with my sister, but I don't know how. We talk every day and lately... I dread it.
